reason: Confirmed: Bundi was a founding member of the Rajasthan Union formed 25 March 1948. Rajasthan overprints on Bundi's 1947 issue (native-character handstamps, later machine overprints in 1949) confirm direct postal succession. File period Bundi 1894–1948 consistent.
notes: Bundi joined the Rajasthan Union on 25 March 1948; Rajasthan overprints hand-stamped in native characters on Bundi's 1947 issue confirm direct postal succession.

reason: Confirmed: Kishangarh was a founding member of the Rajasthan Union, 25 March 1948. Kishangarh's Rajasthan handstamp overprints are the same type as Bundi's. File period ends 1947, but political succession postdating the final issue is normal and not an inconsistency.
notes: Kishangarh acceded to the Rajasthan Union on 25 March 1948; Rajasthan hand-stamped overprints in native characters (same type as Bundi's) on almost all Kishangarh issues confirm a direct postal handover.

reason: Confirmed: Greater Rajasthan formed 30 March 1949 absorbing Jaipur, Jodhpur, Bikaner and Jaisalmer. Eleven Jaipur stamps machine-overprinted 'Rajasthan' in native and English characters. File period Jaipur 1904–1948 consistent.
notes: Jaipur merged into Greater Rajasthan on 30 March 1949; a set of eleven Jaipur stamps machine-overprinted in native and English characters confirms postal succession.

reason: DATE CORRECTED. Proposer gave 1948-04-18, which is the wrong wave of integration: 18 April 1948 is when Udaipur/Mewar joined to form the United State of Rajasthan. Dungarpur was already a founding member of the Rajasthan Union created 25 March 1948 (Kota, Bundi, Jhalawar, Tonk, Kishangarh, Pratapgarh, Dungarpur, Banswara, Shahpura). Confidence held at medium: no known Rajasthan overprints on Dungarpur, so the postal handover remains inferred from political succession.
notes: Dungarpur was a founding member of the Rajasthan Union formed 25 March 1948 (not the 18 April 1948 United State of Rajasthan, which is when Udaipur joined). Issuing until 1948. Political succession certain; no known Rajasthan overprints on Dungarpur, so postal handover is inferred rather than attested.

reason: CONTESTED FLAG ADDED. Relationship, direction and date are defensible (Morvi acceded to the United State of Kathiawar on 15 February 1948; file period Morvi 1931–1948 vs Soruth 1864–1949 supports the direction). Flagged contested because the successor node conflates two distinct administrations: the Junagadh state post (issuing from 1864) and the United State of Saurashtra that absorbed the Kathiawar states. There is also a date seam — political accession was 1948-02-15, but the postal extension of Soruth stamps to the union states tracks Junagadh's own merger into the USS on 1949-01-20.
notes: Morvi was a live Kathiawar postal administration absorbed into the United State of Kathiawar/Saurashtra on 15 February 1948; Soruth stamps, at first issued in Junagadh only, were later extended to the Saurashtra Union including Morvi. Contested: the 'Soruth (Saurashtra)' node conflates the Junagadh state post with the United State of Saurashtra administration, and the postal extension follows Junagadh's 20 January 1949 merger rather than the February 1948 political accession.

reason: CONTESTED FLAG ADDED; confidence deliberately held at low. Jasdan joined the Saurashtra Union in 1948 and Soruth (Saurashtra) stamps were later extended to it, but this rests on thin single-source evidence. reason: Names exact. Confirmed: the Neapolitan Provinces set was issued 14 February 1861, matching both the proposed date and the file's Naples [1858-1861] / Neapolitan Provinces [1861-1862]. Regime_change is the right type — the Bourbon administration was displaced, not merged or annexed.
notes: After Garibaldi's conquest and annexation to Sardinia, the Bourbon Naples issues were replaced by the Neapolitan Provinces (Provincie Napoletane) series.

reason: Type, contested flag and confidence all wrong. The catalogue entity 'Two Sicilies' spans Naples AND Sicily; only the mainland half was succeeded by the Neapolitan Provinces, while Sicily followed its own path. That makes this a partition, not a regime_change, and it is a genuinely contested many-to-many case that was flagged false. Confidence downgraded medium->low: the file separately lists 'Naples' [1858-1861], 'Sicily' [1859-1860] and 'Two Sicilies' [1858-1861] as overlapping entities, so this edge duplicates the Naples->Neapolitan Provinces edge and may double-count depending on how the loader treats the redundant listing.
notes: The 'Two Sicilies' listing overlaps both the Naples and Sicily listings in this file. Its mainland postal administration was replaced by the Neapolitan Provinces issues on 14 Feb 1861, but the Sicilian half is not covered by this edge. Contested and many-to-many; consider whether Two Sicilies should carry succession edges at all given Naples and Sicily are listed independently.

reason: Both type and date are wrong. Date: confirmed that the changeover to Sardinian stamps in Naples took place on 15 September 1862, not 1862-01-01. Type: 'annexation' is a category error here — the political annexation to Sardinia happened in 1860/61, BEFORE the Neapolitan Provinces series even existed, so the transitional series cannot have been annexed by Sardinia. What actually happened is that the transitional issues were absorbed into the kingdom-wide Sardinian/Italian series, which is a merger. Confidence downgraded to medium given the awkward direction (Sardinia [1851-1863] long predates the predecessor).
notes: The transitional Neapolitan Provinces series gave way to the Sardinian (soon Italian) issues used kingdom-wide on 15 Sept 1862. Note the successor pre-dates the predecessor in the file (Sardinia 1851-1863), so this is a postal-administration absorption, not a chronological state succession; the political annexation had already occurred in 1860-61.

reason: Date, contested flag and confidence all need correction. The proposed 1860-10-21 is the plebiscite date, not the annexation — Sicily was formally annexed to Sardinia by decree in December 1860 (sources confirm Sicily became part of the Kingdom of Sardinia in November/December 1860, after the 21 Oct plebiscite). More importantly this edge is contested and was flagged false: Sicily's postal succession actually ran through the Neapolitan Provinces issues, so Sicily->Sardinia skips an intermediate that IS present in this file, and the proposer's own note admits the intermediate usage is 'catalogued inconsistently'. Confidence downgraded medium->low.
notes: Sicilian Bourbon issues demonetised after annexation. Direct Sicily->Sardinia skips the Neapolitan Provinces intermediate, which is listed in this file; consider re-pointing this edge to 'Neapolitan Provinces' instead. Contested and catalogued inconsistently.

notes: Duchy annexed to Sardinia after the 1860 plebiscite; Sardinian stamps replaced the Provisional Government issues.

reason: Names exact. reason: Both names exact; Xinhai Revolution, ROC proclaimed 1 Jan 1912. Matches file periods (Empire 1878-1912, Republic 1912-1949) exactly. Imperial stamps overprinted for the Republic — direct postal continuity.
notes: Xinhai Revolution; Imperial Chinese Post continued as the Chinese (Republic) Post with overprinted Imperial stamps.

reason: Relationship is real but the edge is weak on three counts. (1) The file gives 'Treaty ports' the period [?-present], which contradicts any 1898 termination — this heading is an umbrella grouping, not a single issuer. (2) The successor 'Chinese Empire' [1878-1912] already existed for 20 years before the alleged succession, so this is absorption, not a merger of peers. (3) Many-to-many: this umbrella covers Amoy, Chefoo, Chinkiang, Chunking, Foochow, Hankow, Ichang, Kiukiang, Nanking, Shanghai, Wuhu, each with its own closure. Downgraded high->low and flagged contested.
notes: Umbrella grouping, not a single issuer. Imperial edict closed the treaty-port local posts; mails taken over by the Imperial Chinese Post. Date approximate and the file's [?-present] period is inconsistent with termination in 1898.

reason: Names exact and the relationship is well attested — the Shanghai Local Post was absorbed by the Imperial Chinese Post in 1898, consistent with the file's Shanghai [1865-1898]. Downgraded high->medium: sources agree on the year 1898 but I could not confirm the precise 1 Feb day, and the successor predates the predecessor's demise by 20 years, so 'merger' is a loose fit for what was an absorption.
notes: Shanghai Local Post (1865-1898), largest and oldest of the local posts, absorbed into the Imperial Chinese Post. Year 1898 solid; exact day not verified.

reason: Names exact; PRC proclaimed 1 Oct 1949, matching the file's Chinese Republic [1912-1949] / Chinese People's Republic [1949-present]. Correctly flagged contested — the ROC never conceded and continues to issue.
notes: CPC victory on the mainland; PRC postal administration replaced the ROC's on the mainland. Contested because the ROC never conceded and continues to issue.

reason: Type is wrong. 'partition' implies the ROC split into pieces, but no territory divided at this event — the same government and the same postal administration physically relocated to Taipei on 7 Dec 1949 and kept issuing. The file's periods (Chinese Republic [1912-1949] -> Chinese Nationalist Republic [1949-present]) describe a catalogue heading change for a continuing issuer, i.e. 'renamed'. Contested flag correctly retained: the polity does not regard itself as having been succeeded at all. The partition of China as a whole is already carried by the Chinese Republic -> Chinese People's Republic edge; encoding it twice double-counts.
notes: ROC government and postal administration relocated to Taipei 7 Dec 1949; philatelically catalogued as a separate continuing issuer. Same polity in its own view, hence contested.

reason: Names exact; file periods Japanese Taiwan (Formosa) [1945-1945] -> Taiwan [1945-1949] align with Retrocession Day, 25 Oct 1945. 'annexation' is the right call rather than 'restored': Formosa was ceded to Japan outright in 1895, so the ROC was not restoring its own prior administration but taking over a territory it had never postally administered.
notes: Japanese surrender of Formosa; ROC took over the island's postal service and issued Taiwan provincial overprints.

reason: Two errors. (1) Date: Manchukuo dissolved 18 Aug 1945, not 15 Aug — Puyi read the abdication edict on 17 Aug and the dissolution is recorded as 18 Aug; 15 Aug is the Japanese surrender broadcast, a different event. (2) Type: 'annexation' is wrong-headed. Manchuria was Chinese territory detached by a Japanese puppet state in 1932; China did not annex it, it recovered it. 'restored' fits the option set. Downgraded high->medium because the file's North Eastern Provinces [1946-1948] begins over a year after the dissolution, so this is not a clean handover.
notes: Manchukuo dissolved 18 Aug 1945 with the Japanese surrender; territory reincorporated into China and served by ROC North Eastern Provinces issues from 1946. Gap of over a year between the two issuing periods.

reason: Names exact; Manchukuo founded 1 Mar 1932, matching the file's Manchukuo [1932-1945]. Kirin and Heilungkiang [1927-1931] is the immediate philatelic predecessor in that territory, with only a short gap. 'annexation' is apt here — unlike the reverse case, this genuinely was territory being taken. Medium confidence is appropriately calibrated.
notes: Provinces covered by the Kirin & Heilungkiang issues were incorporated into the Japanese-sponsored Manchukuo state, whose own post replaced them.

reason: Relationship is real but the note is factually off and the confidence was inflated. The file gives North Eastern Provinces [1946-1948] — those issues had already ceased by the end of 1948, when Manchuria fell to the PLA (Shenyang, 2 Nov 1948), nearly a year before the PRC existed. The interval was covered by Communist liberation-area issues that have no entity in this file, so the edge papers over a real gap. Date left at 1949-10-01 because the successor cannot predate its own founding. Downgraded medium->low.
notes: NEP issues actually ended in 1948 when Manchuria fell to the PLA, almost a year before the PRC was founded; the intervening liberation-area issues are not represented in this file. Date is the PRC founding, not the actual transfer.

reason: Names exact; the file's Sinkiang [1915-1949] terminates exactly where this edge places it. Xinjiang's peaceful incorporation (late Sept 1949) sits immediately before the PRC founding, so the 1 Oct 1949 date is defensible. Medium confidence appropriate.
